stories.headline: Measles case confirmed at Denver International Airport; free vaccinations offered in Littleton
stories.summary:
A person with a confirmed measles case traveled through Denver International Airport on Saturday, Feb. 21, and attended a church service in Littleton while infectious, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment announced Tuesday. The passenger arrived on Frontier flight 1279 from Miami at 9:15 a.m. and attended St. Mary's Catholic Church, 6853 S. Prince St. in Littleton, between 9:30 a.m. and 2 p.m. Sunday before departing DIA again that evening.

Anyone at gate A83 between 9:15 a.m. and noon Saturday, at the church between 9:30 a.m. and 2 p.m. Sunday, or at DIA between 9:30 p.m. Sunday and 1:30 a.m. Monday may have been exposed. State officials noted that because the traveler was fully vaccinated, the risk of transmission is lower but still possible. Passengers on both Frontier flights will be contacted directly by their state or local public health agency.

Public health officials offered free measles vaccinations Wednesday at the Arapahoe County Public Health Department, 4857 S. Broadway in Englewood, and at St. Mary's Catholic Church. Officials said vaccination within 72 hours of exposure may prevent the disease or reduce its severity.

Colorado recorded 36 measles cases in 2025, the highest single-year total since at least 1996, with most cases among unvaccinated individuals. One measles case has been confirmed in Colorado in 2026, also in Arapahoe County, according to the CDPHE measles dashboard.

stories.headline: Grass fire near Thornton injures five, forces evacuations and I-25 closures
stories.summary:
A rapidly spreading grass fire broke out Feb. 25 at 84th Avenue and Huron Street in Thornton under Red Flag conditions, prompting large-scale evacuations and temporary closure of I-25 due to dense smoke that severely reduced visibility. The blaze grew quickly eastward along a greenbelt between a residential neighborhood and a commercial zone before being fully contained at approximately 10 acres by 2:07 p.m.

Thornton Fire Chief Stephen Kelley said the fire required a multi-agency response involving departments from Thornton, Westminster, Adams County, North Denver Metro, Northglenn, Federal Heights, South Adams, and others. A nearby high school was evacuated as a precaution, and law enforcement went door-to-door to alert residents. Crews remained on scene overnight to monitor for reignition.

Five people were injured in connection with the fire, including four firefighters and one civilian. Kelley said all injuries were non-critical. No residential structures were destroyed, though some businesses along the fire's southern edge sustained damage that was not believed to be structural.

"This was a dynamic, rapidly spreading event that challenged many of the departments in the north area," Kelley said at a post-fire press conference. The cause of the fire remains under investigation.

stories.headline: Man arrested in Kansas on suspicion of murder in Denver Valentine's Day shooting
stories.summary:
A 20-year-old man suspected of fatally shooting another man at a Denver party on Valentine's Day was arrested Friday in Kansas, Denver police said. Yeanbraiker Yriarte-Valera was booked into the Wyandotte County Detention Center in Kansas City on a Denver homicide warrant and was being held there as of Tuesday afternoon.

Denver police responded to the shooting at approximately 5:15 a.m. Feb. 14 in the 1500 block of West Maple Avenue, where they found a man dead at the scene and a woman shot in the ankle. Investigators believe a fight at a party escalated into a shooting involving at least two suspects. Yriarte-Valera is believed to have fired the shot that killed the male victim.

Yriarte-Valera faces charges of first-degree murder, four counts of attempted first-degree murder, four counts of first-degree assault, and a violent crime sentence enhancer, according to Denver court records. A second suspect, whose bullet allegedly struck the female victim, had not been publicly identified as of Tuesday.

Yriarte-Valera's first Colorado court date had not been scheduled as of Tuesday. Anyone with information is asked to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867.

stories.headline: Two defendants plead guilty to second-degree murder in 2024 Denver Aquarium drive-by shooting
stories.summary:
Jason Trujillo, 19, and Antonio Vasquez, 21, have each pleaded guilty to second-degree murder in connection with the February 2024 drive-by shooting that killed 19-year-old Dacien Salazar outside the Denver Aquarium, the Denver District Attorney's Office confirmed. Both defendants are scheduled to be sentenced March 6.

Salazar and two friends had traveled from Pueblo to the aquarium on Valentine's Day 2024 when a person wearing a black mask in a black sedan opened fire on them outside the attraction. Salazar was taken to a hospital, where he died. Investigators connected the aquarium shooting to a separate shooting in Pueblo and used surveillance footage, cell phone records, and an anonymous tip to identify and arrest the suspects in May 2024.

Triujillo and Vasquez had originally been charged with first-degree murder. Vasquez pleaded guilty Dec. 19, 2025, and Trujillo followed on Jan. 16, 2026, according to the DA's office. The case drew significant public attention because of its location and the victims' ages.

The guilty pleas resolve a case that investigators say stemmed from threats made to Salazar over Facebook before the shooting. Sentencing is set for March 6 in Denver District Court.

stories.headline: Denver and Colorado business groups welcome Supreme Court tariff ruling, call it temporary relief
stories.summary:
Business leaders across the Denver metro and Colorado responded quickly to the U.S. Supreme Court's 6-3 decision striking down the Trump administration's sweeping tariffs imposed under emergency powers law, calling the ruling a source of clarity and stability. The Denver Metro Chamber of Commerce and the Colorado Chamber of Commerce both issued statements welcoming the decision while cautioning that the trade policy landscape remains uncertain.

"The Court's decision provides important clarity and helps establish the defined lanes needed for policymakers to work together toward thoughtful, consistent trade policies," said J.J. Ament, president and CEO of the Denver Metro Chamber of Commerce. Colorado industries affected by the tariffs had included wheat producers, bicycle retailers, and automotive dealerships, among others.

Despite the ruling, some business leaders cautioned that relief may be short-lived. Matthew Groves, president and CEO of one Colorado trade group, said the administration retains multiple other legal avenues to impose tariffs and described the ruling as "temporary relief." Hours after the decision, President Trump announced a 10% global tariff rate via executive order and said the court's ruling "doesn't matter" given available alternatives.

The Colorado Automobile Dealers Association said it "supports anything that will contribute to making vehicles more affordable for consumers." Colorado Chamber of Commerce President and CEO Loren Furman said members across sectors had experienced significant operational impacts from the tariffs, including supply chain disruptions and increased overhead costs.

stories.headline: Laura Clellan confirmed as first female director of Colorado Parks and Wildlife since 2011 merger
stories.summary:
Colorado Parks and Wildlife commissioners voted unanimously Monday to confirm Laura Clellan as the agency's permanent director, ending a search that drew approximately 150 applicants. Clellan, who had served as acting director since Dec. 1, becomes the first woman to lead CPW since the parks and wildlife divisions merged in 2011.

Clellan brings more than three decades of military service, including multiple overseas deployments, and previously served as adjutant general and executive director of the Colorado Department of Military and Veterans Affairs. She achieved the rank of major general in the Colorado Army National Guard. She also has prior experience with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service in Lakewood.

Clellan assumes the role at a contentious moment for the agency. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service has halted Colorado's wolf reintroduction program, stakeholder groups are battling over furbearer management rules, and some hunting advocacy groups have raised concerns that the Polis administration is working to marginalize their interests in agency appointments and policy decisions.

Conservation groups and hunting advocates offered cautiously optimistic assessments of the selection. Rob Edward of the Rocky Mountain Wolf Project said his organization has "nothing but high expectations" for Clellan, while Dan Gates of Coloradans for Responsible Wildlife Management said his group is confident she will provide equitable transparency and accountability in leading the agency.

stories.headline: Denver Downtown Development Authority hires Urban Land Institute to chart future of Denver Pavilions
stories.summary:
The Denver Downtown Development Authority has engaged the Urban Land Institute to assess the future of the Denver Pavilions mall and its adjacent parking lots, paying the real estate advisory organization $100,000 for an advisory panel visit scheduled for April 13-17. The panel, which will consist of five or six outside-region experts, will produce a report with redevelopment recommendations approximately one month after the visit.

The DDDA purchased the 350,000-square-foot mall at 500 16th St. in December for $37 million and acquired two nearby parking lots along 15th Street for $23 million from New York-based Brookfield Properties. The property is approximately 60% leased and generates $5.3 million in annual rent, with the parking assets adding another $3.2 million annually. Gart Properties, the former owner that had defaulted on its loan, continues to manage the mall for $379,000.

Ahead of the ULI panel visit, the DDDA has issued two requests for proposals — one seeking a repositioning vision from retail and entertainment leasing specialists, the other seeking architecture and urban planning firms to reimagine the property and adjacent parking lots as a "catalytic centerpiece of a revitalized Upper Downtown." Submissions are due March 13.

"My hope is that we transfer this asset to the private sector as soon as possible and let them execute their vision," said Bill Mosher, Denver's chief projects officer. The DDDA has emphasized preserving flexibility and avoiding long-term lease commitments until a redevelopment direction is established.

stories.headline: Historic Loretto Heights administration building in Denver to become 50 rent-to-own apartments in $70M project
stories.summary:
Developer Commún plans to convert the historic Administration Building at the former Loretto Heights campus in southwest Denver into 50 rent-to-own apartments, part of a broader $70 million campus redevelopment effort. The project aims to create an affordable pathway to homeownership in a city where such models are rare.

The first two floors of the building are planned to host community programming, including a business incubator, while the upper floors are outfitted as affordable residential units. A historic chapel that is part of the building will be preserved and continue operating as a wedding and event venue, maintaining the site's cultural character.

The Loretto Heights campus has been the subject of redevelopment efforts for several years, with community advocates pushing for preservation of the historic structures and affordable housing components. The rent-to-own model being proposed by Commún is intended to offer residents a path toward building equity rather than remaining perpetual renters.

The project comes as Denver faces persistently high construction costs — ranked second-highest nationally according to a 2026 report by the Beck Group — driven in part by luxury projects and complex downtown developments.

stories.headline: Globeville mixed-income housing development breaks ground on $132M Denver project
stories.summary:
Evergreen Real Estate Group and Globeville Redevelopment Partners have broken ground on a $132 million mixed-use development that will transform a city-owned parcel on the site of a former car dealership in Denver's Globeville neighborhood. The project will create housing for families earning between 30% and 80% of the area median income.

Globeville is one of Denver's most historically underinvested neighborhoods, situated between major highway corridors and industrial land uses. The redevelopment of a formerly city-owned, commercially blighted parcel into income-restricted housing represents a significant investment in a community that has faced environmental and economic challenges for decades.

The mixed-income structure is designed to serve a range of lower- to moderate-income households, a model that advocates say helps avoid concentrating poverty while expanding the city's supply of deed-restricted affordable units. Details on the unit count and timeline were not fully disclosed in available reporting.

The project adds to a pipeline of affordable and mixed-income housing development across the Denver metro as the region continues to grapple with a shortage of units accessible to lower-wage workers and families.

stories.headline: Heat pump installations in Colorado more than doubled in 2025, marking industry tipping point
stories.summary:
The number of heat pumps installed across the service territories of five major Colorado utilities more than doubled in 2025 to 14,225 units, a twelvefold increase over three years, according to utility data and industry representatives. Xcel Energy alone issued 10,640 heat pump rebates worth $57 million last year, with Denver, Boulder, and Littleton among the most active markets.

Industry executives and contractors say 2025 marked an inflection point at which heat pumps transitioned from a niche technology to a broadly competitive one, as contractors became more skilled at installation and better equipped to stack utility and federal rebates to reduce upfront costs. Heat pumps can range from $15,000 to $30,000 depending on system size, but rebates have significantly narrowed the cost gap with traditional furnaces and air conditioners.

Nationally, 3.64 million heat pumps were sold in the U.S. in 2025, surpassing the 3.28 million natural gas and oil furnaces sold in the same period, according to the Air-Conditioning, Heating and Refrigeration Institute. The Platte River Power Authority, which serves Fort Collins, Longmont, Loveland, and Estes Park, saw its heat pump rebates nearly quadruple in 2025 to 1,256.

The Energy Efficiency Business Coalition, a trade group, has set a goal of heat pumps capturing 50% to 80% of the total heating and cooling market in Colorado by 2030. The technology operates in both heating and cooling modes and is engineered to function even in subzero temperatures.

stories.headline: MSU Denver launches fellowship to support and retain Black male educators in Denver and Aurora schools
stories.summary:
Metropolitan State University of Denver has launched the Black Male Educator Fellowship, nicknamed "The Den," aimed at supporting and retaining Black male teachers working in Denver Public Schools and Aurora Public Schools. The fellowship is a collaborative effort among MSU Denver's Call Me MiSTER program, the university's Office of Education Solutions, Denver and Aurora Public Schools, and the Colorado Men of Color Collaborative.

The program is designed to address the acute isolation many Black male educators experience in Colorado schools. According to the National Association of State Boards of Education, 40% of Colorado school districts have no teacher of color, and 62% have no Black male teacher. Program director Dr. Rashad Anderson said the fellowship aims to build professional community and reduce the isolation that often drives educators from the classroom.

"I know how isolating it can be, particularly for Black men," Anderson said. "In the Denver community, we're 6% of the population in the state, and for many educators, they're the only Black teacher in their school building." Anderson said the expectation that Black educators will serve as sole disciplinary or counseling resources for all students of color adds additional burden to an already demanding job.

The fellowship offers professional development, networking, and community-building opportunities. Program participant Kaheem Heath, an aspiring teacher, said he has never had a Black male teacher in his own schooling and sees the fellowship as a mechanism for inspiring others to enter the profession and serve as role models for students who lack them.

stories.headline: Broncos reunite with John Morton as offensive passing-game coordinator ahead of Payton's fourth season
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have hired John Morton as offensive passing-game coordinator, a source confirmed to the Denver Post on Sunday, completing the team's key offensive staff restructuring heading into Sean Payton's fourth season. Morton previously served in the same role with Denver in 2023 and 2024 before leaving for an offensive coordinator position with the Detroit Lions.

Morton's return to Denver's building was already underway before the formal hire. After the Lions stripped Morton of play-calling duties midseason and then released him following a 9-8 finish, Payton brought him back as a consultant during the Broncos' playoff run. In that capacity, Morton helped install a goal-line play for offensive tackle Frank Crum that resulted in a touchdown in Denver's 33-30 overtime divisional win over the Buffalo Bills.

The hire completes an offseason staff overhaul triggered by Payton's decision to fire offensive coordinator Joe Lombardi two days after Denver's AFC Championship Game loss. Payton subsequently elevated former quarterbacks coach Davis Webb to offensive coordinator, promoted offensive quality control coach Logan Kilgore to quarterbacks coach, and hired Ronald Curry as wide receivers coach.

Morton's relationship with Payton stretches back to 2006, when Morton served as receivers coach in Payton's first year with the New Orleans Saints. Whether Morton will feed play concepts to Payton directly on gamedays or work through newly appointed play-caller Davis Webb remains to be determined.
